selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: I did not confirm a GreatFrontEnd page directly focused on BigInt precision, arithmetic mixing rules, and JSON serialization boundaries. Nearby general JavaScript pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct BigInt references.

competitors.label: MDN BigInt
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for BigInt behavior, syntax, comparisons, and arithmetic rules.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative reference for BigInt syntax, supported operators, and comparison semantics.
theirGaps: The reference format is less centered on one product scenario such as 64-bit IDs and JSON serialization boundaries.

realWorldUseCases: A payment platform ingests 64-bit transaction IDs from multiple services.
followUpConfusion: JSON.stringify does not serialize BigInt by default and throws.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains BigInt correctly, but our page turns it into a production-safe-integer and serialization decision instead of staying reference-first.
actionableExamples: MDN still provides strong syntax and operator examples, so the actionable-example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the two follow-ups interviewers often ask next: arithmetic mixing with Number and JSON/API boundaries.

actionableExamples: Add one explicit BigInt-to-DB or API-contract example if we later want a clearer example edge over the canonical docs.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical BigInt reference, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it frames BigInt through precision loss, transaction-ID safety, and JSON boundary failures.

competitors.label: javascript.info BigInt
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info is a strong topic-direct explainer for BigInt operations, conversions, and caveats.
theirStrengths: It is a focused tutorial on BigInt arithmetic, conversions, and operator behavior.
theirGaps: It is less interview-shaped and less explicitly anchored to serialization or product-identifier boundaries.

realWorldUseCases: JSON contracts usually keep large integers as strings at the boundary, then parse to bigint inside the app and convert back to string when serializing.
followUpConfusion: Do not mix Number and BigInt in arithmetic.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info is direct and practical on the language behavior, so the real-world criterion stays close.
actionableExamples: Its operator examples are strong, so the example criterion remains tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page does more to answer the likely follow-up about JSON serialization and API contracts, not only arithmetic rules.

actionableExamples: If this comparison still feels close later, add one explicit mixed sort/comparator snippet so the actionable example edge is stronger than the language tutorial baseline.

competitors.winReason: javascript.info is a strong language explainer, but our page now wins the interview calibration because it ties BigInt to real JSON/API boundary contracts and still answers the follow-up traps around mixed arithmetic.

competitors.label: BigInt: arbitrary-precision integers in JavaScript
competitors.selectionReason: The V8 team article is a strong implementation-oriented explainer for why BigInt exists and what problem it solves.
theirStrengths: It explains the precision problem and the language motivation behind BigInt very clearly.
theirGaps: It is more engine- and language-motivation-focused than interview-focused on serialization and mixed-type traps.

realWorldUseCases: A payment platform ingests 64-bit transaction IDs from multiple services.
followUpConfusion: JSON.stringify does not serialize BigInt by default and throws.

realWorldUseCases: The V8 article explains why BigInt exists, but our page is more directly tied to application-level integer-ID and API-boundary problems.
actionableExamples: It still provides useful language examples, so the actionable-example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page covers the operational follow-ups more directly: cannot mix Number and BigInt in arithmetic, division truncates, and JSON conversion is explicit.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one compact comparison example around BigInt sorting or API parsing to strengthen the example edge.

competitors.winReason: The V8 article is strong on why BigInt exists, but our page is better for this interview question because it ties BigInt to practical ID safety, mixed-arithmetic traps, and serialization boundaries.
